root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

probability spaces

Table of contents

  1. The frequentist interpretation
  2. A first look at the axiomatic interpretation
  3. A crash course in set theory
  4. Probability spaces
  5. The big picture: How do we use probability spaces and distributions?
  6. Discrete and uniform probability spaces

The frequentist interpretation

We often make probabilistic statements in our everyday conversation, such as, “I think there’s a one-in-four chance that it will rain tomorrow,” or, “I have a 38.47% chance of failing the final exam in my class.” Such statements attempt to quantify our subjective belief in some event occurring, but there’s no real mathematical basis for obtaining the specific probability values. For example, could you imagine any realistic method that a person might use to rigorously and objectively obtain the exact probability value of 38.47% mentioned above?!?! Yeah, not likely.

On the other hand, we may try to avoid appeals to subjective opinions and assessments and make probability theory purely mathematical by appealing to long-run relative frequencies. For example, suppose that you roll a fair six-sided die:

What is the probability $P(4)$ that you roll a four? I think most people would answer

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6}. \end{equation}

In fact, for any whole number $x$ with $1\leq x \leq 6$, most of us would say:

\begin{equation}\notag P(x) = \frac{1}{6}. \end{equation}

If somebody asked us to explain our value for $P(x)$, we might say something like:

“Well, since the die is fair, there’s an equal chance of rolling any number. And since there are six possible numbers that we could roll, the probability of rolling any one particular number is one in six.”

This is a somewhat satisfying explanation, but it’s still not entirely justified by mathematics.

Here’s an alternative explanation that’s a little more rigorous: Suppose that we roll the die many times over and over, and that we record the result of each roll. We then compute the following ratio:

\begin{equation}\notag \text{relative frequency of $4$s} = \frac{\text{number of $4$s rolled}}{\text{total number of rolls}}. \end{equation}

If the number of rolls is large, what might you expect this ratio to be close to?

To answer this, let’s have a computer do the rolling for us! Here are the results:

Along the horizontal axis you see the values of $n$, where $n$ is the total number of rolls; along the vertical axis, you see the values of the relative frequency of $4$s. For example, when the computer rolled the die $n=40$ times, it appears that the relative frequency was a hair larger than $0.2$. But do you notice that as the number of rolls $n$ increases, the relative frequency of $4$s appears to stabilize along the red dashed line? Let’s have the computer go even further, rolling the die up to $n=1000$ times:

Now we can really see that the relative frequency of $4$s is approaching the red dashed line—but this line just happens to be at a value of $1/6 = 0.1\bar{6}$!

This demonstration suggests that if we wanted a semi-rigorous mathematical explanation for the value

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6}, \end{equation}

we might say that the fraction $1/6$ is the limiting value of the relative frequency of $4$s, as the number of rolls $n$ goes off to $\infty$.

This conceptual framework has a name:

Definition. The conceptualization of probability as a long-run relative frequency is called the frequentist interpretation of probability.

The frequentist interpretation, while it has a certain level of pleasing mathematical precision, does not prove to be entirely satisfactory or appropriate in all probabilistic scenarios. For example, suppose that we believe there is a one-in-four chance that it will rain tomorrow; in symbols, this statement would read as:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{rain}) = \frac{1}{4}. \end{equation}

But how would we interpret the ratio $1/4$ as a long-run relative frequency? We would have to imagine that it is possible to re-live “tomorrow” over and over again, and to count the number of “tomorrows” that it rains. Then, if $P(\text{rain})$ really is $1/4$, then we would expect the relative frequency

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{ \text{number of times it rains}}{\text{number of tomorrows}} \end{equation}

to approach $1/4$ as the number of “tomorrows” goes to infinity. But this is clearly absurd! How could we possibly re-live “tomorrow” over and over?!

A first look at the axiomatic interpretation

So, as you can see, interpreting exactly what probability is can be a messy affair, with certain interpretations and conceptualizations working well in certain scenarios, while failing miserably in others. The way out of this mess is to leave the meaning of probability to the philosophers, and to instead focus on finding rules for handling and manipulating probabilities that we can all agree on. This leads to something called the axiomatic interpretation of probability. While this interpretation is completely rigorous and mathematical, it can be a bit abstract the first time you see it.

In this setting, you can think of the fancy word axiom as a synonym for rule or law.

Let’s first agree that all probabilities, no matter what they really mean, should be represented by numbers between $0$ and $1$. So, our first axiom is:

(Informal) Probability Axiom 1. Probabilities are represented by non-negative numbers.

The reason that this axiom is called “informal” (for now) is because it will appear in a much more precise version later, when we discuss probability spaces.

This axiom simply says that all probabilities are $\geq 0$; but remember, we actually wanted:

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \leq (\text{probability}) \leq 1. \end{equation}

The first axiom captures the inequality $0 \leq (\text{probability})$, so we’ll need a second axiom to capture the other inequality $(\text{probability})\leq 1$. But, as I will explain later, it is actually more expedient to instead assume the following:

(Informal) Probability Axiom 2. The probability of any event occuring is $1$.

Again, it is not clear exactly how this axiom implies that all probabilities are $\leq 1$, but I’ll explain this later. In the context of the die-rolling scenario from the previous section, this axiom says that the probability of rolling any number between $1$ and $6$ is $1 = 100\%$. We can all agree on that, right?

Now, we only need one more axiom to develop our theory. To make this third and last axiom seem plausible, let’s continue with our die-rolling scenario. I think all of us would agree that the probability of rolling an odd number is $1/2$, since precisely half of the numbers are odd, and the other half even:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{odd}) = \frac{1}{2}. \end{equation}

A frequentist would confirm this by rolling the die many times, and tracking the long-run relative frequency:

Sure enough, it appears that the relative frequency of rolling an odd is approaching the value $1/2 = 0.5$. And, of course, we still have the value

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6} \end{equation}

from the previous section.

But the question I want to consider now is: What is the probability of rolling a $4$ or an odd? In symbols:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{$4$ or odd}) =\ ? \end{equation}

To answer this question, let’s have a look at three different long-run relative frequencies:

You can see that the relative frequency of the 4s approaches $1/6=0.1\bar{6}$, as we already knew, and also the relative frequency of odds approaches $1/2=0.5$. But notice that the relative frequency of the 4s or odds appears to approach a number between 0.6 and 0.7, perhaps a bit closer to 0.7 than 0.6.

But wait a minute! We know

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6} \quad \text{and} \quad P(\text{odd}) = \frac{1}{2}, \end{equation}

right? Notice that

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{1}{6}+ \frac{1}{2} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.\bar{6}, \end{equation}

so it appears that we have

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{$4$ or odd}) = P(4) + P(\text{odd}). \end{equation}

This equation suggests that the probability of one event happening, or another, is simply the sum of the two individual probabilities. But, crucially, the two events cannot have any outcomes in common, for otherwise this rule does not work. For example, the probability of rolling a 4 or an even number is just $1/2$, not $1/6+1/2$, because the event of rolling a $4$ is included in the event of rolling an even.

I hope all of this makes the third and final axiom seem quite natural:

(Informal) Probability Axiom 3. The probability of one or the other of two disjoint events occurring is the sum of the individual probabilities.

Here, the word disjoint simply means that there is no single outcome that is included in both events. Think of the example above, of rolling a 4, rolling an odd, and rolling an even. The two events

\begin{equation}\notag \{\text{roll a $4$}\}, \quad \{\text{roll an odd}\}, \end{equation}

are disjoint, while the events

\begin{equation}\notag \{\text{roll a $4$}\}, \quad \{\text{roll an even}\}, \end{equation}

are not disjoint, because the first event is entirely contained in the second.

A crash course in set theory

We’ve collected our three axioms that together constitute the axiomatic interpretation of probability. My goal in the rest of this post is to formalize them using the language and objects of set theory. So, hold on for just a minute as we quickly review the theory of sets.

Definition. A set is a set.

Yes, you read that right. It is a laughably circular definition, but my point is simply that a set in the technical sense, for our purposes, is just a set of objects as you would understand in everyday life. There’s nothing deep here; don’t overthink it.

  • Set notation. Sets are usually represented with capital letters, and the members of a set are enclosed in curly braces. For example, \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1, 2, 3\} \end{equation} is the set containing the numbers $1$, $2$, and $3$, while \begin{equation}\notag B = \{ \text{June}, \text{July}, \text{August}\} \end{equation} is the set containing the three summer months. The things inside a set are called elements of the set, and the symbol “$\in$” is used to indicate that something is in a set. For example, I would write \begin{equation}\notag 1 \in A \quad \text{and} \quad \text{June} \in B \end{equation} to indicate that the number $1$ is in the set $A$ and the month June is in the set $B$, while I would write \begin{equation}\notag 4 \notin A \quad \text{and} \quad \text{March} \notin B \end{equation} to indicate that $A$ does not contain $4$ and that $B$ does not contain March.

  • Subsets. Sometimes every element of a set $A$ might be an element in a bigger set $S$. When this happens, we write \begin{equation}\notag A \subset S \end{equation} and say that $A$ is a subset of the set $S$. For example, we have \begin{equation}\notag \{1,2,3\} \subset \{1,2,3,4\}, \end{equation} while \begin{equation}\notag \{1,2,3\} \not\subset \{1,2\} \end{equation} where the diagonal slash through the subset symbol $\subset$ means “not a subset.” If we picture sets as blogs in Venn diagrams, then to say that $A$ is a subset of $S$ means that:

However, if $A$ is not a subset of $S$, then we would draw something like:

  • Unions. If $A$ and $B$ are two sets, the set of elements that are in $A$ or $B$ is called the union of $A$ and $B$ and is denoted $A\cup B$. In terms of a Venn diagram, we have:

For a specific example, suppose \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3\} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \{ 3, \text{June}, \text{July}\}. \end{equation} Then \begin{equation}\notag A \cup B = \{ 1,2,3, \text{June}, \text{July}\}. \end{equation} Notice that even though $3$ appears as an element in both $A$ and $B$, it only appears once in the union $A\cup B$. This is because sets ignore repetition.

  • Intersections. If $A$ and $B$ are two sets, the set of elements that are in $A$ and $B$ is called the intersection of $A$ and $B$ and is denoted $A\cap B$. In terms of a Venn diagram, we have:

With the example sets \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3\} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \{ 3, \text{June}, \text{July}\}. \end{equation} from above, we would have \begin{equation}\notag A \cap B = \{ 3\} \end{equation} since $3$ is the only element that appears in both $A$ and $B$.

  • The empty set. We write $\emptyset$ to denote the empty set, the set with no elements. To see how this set is used, note that if \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3\} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \{ \text{June}, \text{July}, \text{August}\}, \end{equation} then \begin{equation}\notag A \cap B = \emptyset. \end{equation} This equation simply says that the set of elements that are in $A$ and $B$ is empty; there’s no one element that is in both!

This leads us to make a definition of a term that we saw in the previous section:

Definition. Two sets $A$ and $B$ are called disjoint if their intersection is empty, i.e., \begin{equation}\notag A \cap B = \emptyset. \end{equation}

We need one more concept from set theory:

  • Complements. Suppose that $A$ is a subset of a set $S$. Then the complement of $A$ in $S$ is the set of elements that are in $S$, but not in $A$. It is denoted $S\smallsetminus A$. In terms of a Venn diagram:

For a specific example, suppose that \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3 \} \quad \text{and} \quad S = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}. \end{equation} Then \begin{equation}\notag S\smallsetminus A = \{ 4, 5, 6\}. \end{equation}

That’s it for the definitions! We’ll get some further practice with these concepts as we work through the worksheets in class.

Problem Prompt. Now, I would suggest that you tackle Problems 1 and 2 in worksheet.

Before we leave this section, let’s summarize all that we’ve learned:

Summary of set theory.

  1. A set is a set.

  2. $A$ is a subset of $S$, written $A\subset S$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $S$.

  3. The union of two sets $A$ and $B$, written $A\cup B$, is the set of elements that are in $A$ or $B$. Remember that \begin{equation}\notag \cup \leftrightarrow \text{“or”}. \end{equation}

  4. The intersection of two sets $A$ and $B$, written $A\cap B$, is the set of elements that are in $A$ and $B$. Remember that \begin{equation}\notag \cap \leftrightarrow \text{“and”}. \end{equation}

  5. The empty set is the set $\emptyset$ that contains no elements.

  6. Two sets $A$ and $B$ are disjoint if $A\cap B = \emptyset$.

  7. If $A$ is a subset of $S$, the complement of $A$ in $S$, denoted $S \smallsetminus A$, is the set of elements that are in $S$ but not in $A$.

Probability spaces

With the notation and terminology of set theory in hand, we are now ready to assemble and package our axioms of probability into a new mathematical object called a probability space.

But before we launch into the abstract definition, it will be worth reviewing what we’re trying to accomplish. Basically, everything in this post revolves around the following question:

Q: What is probability?

In the first section of this post, we saw the frequentist interpretation of probability, which, with the goal of making probability seem less subjective, conceives of probabilities as long-run relative frequencies. But there are problems with this interpretation, because to compute a long-run relative frequency, you must imagine the event occurring over and over. But clearly this doesn’t make sense in all situations and scenarios!

This suggests that the answer to the fundamental question “What is probability?” might be more difficult to come by than you might have first imagined. So, instead of getting lost in the philosophical weeds struggling to cook up a good answer, we essentially side-step the question:

Q: What is probability?

A: I don’t know. But I do know that probabilities, no matter what they are, should follow a simple set of three rules, or axioms:

  1. Probabilities are all non-negative numbers.

  2. The probability of any event occurring is $1$.

  3. The probability of one or the other of two disjoint events occurring is the sum of the individual probabilities.

In the language of set theory, it is this simple set of three axioms that we will now formalize in the concept of a probability space.

Definition. A probability space consists of three things:

  1. A set $S$ called the sample space.

  2. A collection of subsets of $S$, called events.

  3. A probability measure $P$.

Let’s work through each piece, one by one, beginning with the sample space $S$. You should imagine that $S$ is the set of all outcomes of some process. For example:

  1. In the die-rolling scenario, the sample space would be \begin{equation}\notag S = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}, \end{equation} which consists of all possible outcomes of rolling the die.

  2. Similarly, in a coin-flipping scenario, your sample space would be \begin{equation}\notag S = \{ H, T\}, \end{equation} where $H$ stands for “heads” and $T$ for “tails”.

  3. Suppose you’re a weather forecaster, and it’s your business to predict whether it will rain tomorrow or not. Then your sample space would be \begin{equation}\notag S = \{ \text{rain}, \text{no rain}\}. \end{equation}

  4. Suppose that we flip a coin three times. The sample space of all possible outcomes would be \begin{equation}\notag S = \{HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT \}. \end{equation}

  5. Suppose that we flip a coin until we see a heads. The sample space of all possible outcomes is \begin{equation}\notag S = \{H, TH, TTH, TTTH, TTTTH, \ldots\}. \end{equation}

  6. Suppose that you are conducting an experiment to measure the speed of light, with your measurement being the outcome. The sample space is then the open interval \begin{equation}\notag S = (0,\infty) \subset \bbr \end{equation} since you could (theoretically) obtain any positive number.

Each of these sample spaces consists of the possible outcomes of some process or experiment.

Definition.

  • The elements of a sample space $S$ are called outcomes or sample points. (The textbook favors the second term—I favor the first.)

  • If $s\in S$ is an outcome (or sample point) in a sample space $S$, the corresponding one-element subset $\{s\}$ (of $S$) is called a simple event.

So, one-element subsets of a sample space are called simple events. Larger subsets, however, are just called plain events:

Definition. An event in a sample space $S$ is a subset $A$ of $S$.

This definition is almost correct; there’s a technicality that needs to be added in the case of a “continuous” sample space, like the one in the speed-of-light example above. But we don’t worry about that stuff at this level.

Here are some examples of events from each of the scenarios above, listed in the same order:

  1. The event that we roll an even number is the subset \begin{equation}\notag A = \{2, 4, 6\}. \end{equation}

  2. The only subsets of $S$ are \begin{equation}\notag \emptyset, \{H\}, \{T\}, S, \end{equation} so there aren’t really any interesting events besides the simple ones.

  3. Again, the only subsets of $S$ are \begin{equation}\notag \emptyset, \{\text{rain}\}, \{\text{no rain}\}, S, \end{equation} so, there aren’t really any interesting events besides the simple ones.

  4. The event that we flip exactly two heads is the subset \begin{equation}\notag A = \{HHT, HTH, THH\}. \end{equation}

  5. The event that we flip an even number of tails before we see a head corresponds to the subset \begin{equation}\notag A = \{ H, TTH, TTTTH, TTTTTTH, \ldots\}. \end{equation}

  6. The event that you measure a number between 600 and 1000 (inclusive) is the subset \begin{equation}\notag A = [600, 1000]. \end{equation}

Problem Prompt. For more practice with sample spaces and events, take a look at Problems 3 and 4 in the worksheet.

Before we move on to talk about the third ingredient in a probability space, let me add one more important thing about events:

Properties of events.

  1. If $A$ is an event, then so too is its complement $S\smallsetminus A$.

  2. The entire sample space $S$ itself is always an event.

  3. If $A_1,A_2,A_3,\ldots$ is a sequence of events, then the union \begin{equation}\notag A_1 \cup A_2 \cup A_3 \cup \cdots \end{equation} is also an event.

These properties are essentially here for logical consistency and won’t play a giant role in our course. In deeper treatments of probability theory they become much more important, and they are connected with the technicality I mentioned above regarding what subsets are allowed to be events in a “continuous” probability space. If you want a glimpse of what I’m talking about, look here, but don’t say I didn’t warn you!

Problem Prompt. That being said, there are one or two consequences of these properties of events that will be important for us. You’ll work them out yourself in Problem 5 in the worksheet.

Onward to the third (and final!) ingredient of a probability space—probability measures!

Definition. Let $S$ be a sample space. A probability measure $P$ (also called a probability distribution) is a function that to each event $A$ in $S$ assigns a number $P(A)$, called the probability of $A$, subject to the following axioms:

  1. $P(A) \geq 0$ for all events $A$.

  2. $P(S)=1$.

  3. If $A_1,A_2,A_3,\ldots$ is a sequence of pairwise disjoint events in $S$, then \begin{equation}\notag P(A_1 \cup A_2 \cup A_3 \cup \cdots) = \sum_{i=1}^\infty P(A_i). \end{equation}

We know what it means for two events to be disjoint, but what does it mean for an entire sequence of events to be pairwise disjoint? It means that, given any two distinct indices, the events $A_i$ and $A_j$ are disjoint, i.e.,

\begin{equation}\notag A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset. \end{equation}

Now, if you look at the three defining axioms of a probability measure long enough, you’ll notice that they are the precise set-theoretic manifestations of our three informal axioms for probability! That the first two axioms in each set match up is pretty clear, but it might not be so clear how the third axioms in the sets match each other. We will explore this point in more detail in the worksheets during class.

But for now, just make sure you understand that a probability measure (or distribution!) is nothing but a function which takes events as inputs and spits out probabilities:

Problem Prompt. The third axiom defining a probability measure involves an infinite(!) sum—but what about simpler finite sums? Can this be brought down to earth? Yes! You’ll see how, along with a few other important properties of probability measures, in Problem 6 in the worksheet.

The big picture: How do we use probability spaces and distributions?

As we go through this class, you should imagine that you are filling up your toolkit with various special types of probability spaces and distributions. Then, when you’re confronted with some real-world scenario that you’d like to model, you reach into your toolkit and find the correct probability distribution which fits the scenario. The challenge, of course, is to figure out exactly which one works the best!

This is really hard, so do not despair if it’s difficult at first! Professional statisticians and applied mathematicians spend a huge part of their professional lives struggling with this question!

Discrete and uniform probability spaces

So, let’s begin filling up our probabilistic toolkit!

The first types of probability spaces that we shall study are the discrete ones:

Definition.

  • A sample space $S$ is called discrete if it is finite or countably infinite.

  • A probability space is called discrete if its sample space $S$ is discrete, and all subsets of $S$ are events.

What the heck does “countably infinite” mean?

Definiton (sort of). A set $S$ is countably infinite if, given an infinite amount of time, I could count the elements of $S$ one at a time, counting one element per second.

Of course, this isn’t really a precise mathematical definition, but I think it gets the idea across. It just means that the set $S$ is countably infinite if its elements can be paired off one-by-one with the set of positive whole numbers

\begin{equation}\notag \{1,2,3,\ldots\}. \end{equation}

For example, I could count an infinite sequence of bananas, so this sequence would be considered countably infinite:

On the other hand, any interval of real numbers, including the entire real line $\bbr$ itself, is uncountably infinite. For example, consider the interval $[0,1]$:

If we are counting the numbers in $[0,1]$ from left to right, we would be tempted to choose $0$ as the first number—but what on earth would the second number be?!?! And, even if you managed to think of a good candidate for the second number, you’d never be able to count all numbers in $[0,1]$, one at a time, one per second, even if you had an infinite amount of time. There are actually rigorous mathematical proofs of this!

Q: Ok, cool. So we know what a discrete probability space is—but why should we care about them?

A: One reason (among others), is that the probability measures on discrete probability spaces are really easy to describe.

To help explain, for simplicity let’s first consider the case of a finite sample space consisting of four sample points (also called outcomes):

\begin{equation}\notag S = \{s_1,s_2,s_3,s_4\}. \end{equation}

Any event in $S$ whatsoever can be written as a union of simple events (i.e., one-element subsets); for example, the event

\begin{equation}\notag A = \{s_2,s_3\} \quad \text{can be written as} \quad A = \{s_2\} \cup \{s_3\}. \end{equation}

But, by the third axiom of a probability measure, we have that

\begin{equation}\label{sum1-eqn} P(A) = P(\{s_2\}) + P(\{s_3\}). \end{equation}

Similarly, the event

\begin{equation}\notag B = \{s_1,s_2,s_3\} \quad \text{can be written as} \quad B = \{s_1\} \cup \{s_2\} \cup \{s_3\}, \end{equation}

so another application of the third axiom of a probability measure gives

\begin{equation}\label{sum2-eqn} P(B) = P(\{s_1\}) + P(\{s_2\}) + P(\{s_3\}). \end{equation}

Do you see what this all means? It means that the whole probability measure $P$ is completely determined by the probability that it assigns to the simple events:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\{s_1\}), P(\{s_2\}), P(\{s_3\}), P(\{s_4\}). \end{equation}

So, if you know what these four probabilities are, then you can compute the probability of any event just by using the third axiom of a probability measure.

Now, clearly this isn’t a feature only of four-element sample spaces—this same idea works for any discrete sample space. Moreover, these ideas can also be used to define new probability measures on discrete sample spaces. Everything is summarized (a little abstractly) in:

Probability measures on discrete sample spaces. Let $S$ be a discrete sample space.

  1. If $S$ already has a probability measure $P$, then the probability of any event $A$ is given by the formula \begin{equation}\label{sum-eqn} P(A) = \sum_{s\in A} P(\{s\}), \end{equation} where the sum ranges over all sample points $s\in A$.

  2. Conversely, suppose that we would like to define a probability measure on $S$. Then, all we have to do is assign a probability $P(\{s\})$ (between $0$ and $1$, inclusive) to each simple event $\{s\}$, and then we may compute probabilities via the same formula \eqref{sum-eqn}. However, in order for this to work, we must make sure that the probabilities sum to $1$ over the whole sample space, i.e., we have to check that \begin{equation}\label{whole-eqn} \sum_{s\in S} P(\{s\}) =1. \end{equation}

Notice that if the equation \eqref{whole-eqn} isn’t true, then $P(S)$ would be greater than $1$, which would violate the definition of a probability measure! Thus, we need to be very careful that we select our probabilities so that \eqref{whole-eqn} is indeed true.

For something visual to hold onto, here’s a summary:

OK. Only one more definition before we move on to the worksheet and get our hands dirty:

Definition. A discrete probability space is called a uniform probability space if its sample space $S$ is finite and all simple events have the same probability.

In particular, if $S$ contains $n$ elements and $A\subset S$ is an event, then \begin{equation}\notag P(A) = \frac{\text{number of elements in $A$}}{n}. \end{equation}

Here’s a visual to help remember uniform probability spaces:

Problem Prompt. Whew! This was a lot! Head on over to the worksheet and finish with Problems 7, 8 and 9.
